request.question: What number did the song "Reapers"  by Muse reach on Switzerland's main music sales chart?

title: Swiss Hitparade
List of Gaon Digital Chart number ones of 2017
Regional Mexican Albums
Reapers (song)

List of Gaon Digital Chart number ones of 2010

Bardeux
sentences: The Swiss Hitparade (German: Schweizer Hitparade ) are Switzerland's main music sales charts.
The charts are a record of the highest-selling singles and albums in various genres in Switzerland.
The Gaon Digital Chart is a chart that ranks the best-performing singles in South Korea.
Managed by the domestic Ministry of Culture, Sports and Tourism (MCST), its data is compiled by the Korea Music Content Industry Association and published by the Gaon Music Chart.
The ranking is based collectively on each single's download sales, stream count, and background music use.
In mid-2008, the Recording Industry Association of Korea ceased publishing music sales data.
The MCST established a process to collect music sales in 2009, and began publishing its data with the introduction of the Gaon Music Chart the following February.
With the creation of the Gaon Digital Chart, digital data for individual songs was provided in the country for the first time.
Gaon provides weekly (listed from Sunday to Saturday), monthly and yearly charts.
Below is a list of singles that topped the weekly and monthly charts.
The Regional Mexican Albums is a genre-specific record chart published weekly by "Billboard" magazine in the United States.

The chart was established in June 1985 and originally listed the top twenty-five best-selling albums of mariachi, tejano, norteño, banda and duranguense, which are frequently considered regional Mexican.

The genre is considered by musicologist as being "the biggest-selling Latin music genre in the United States", and represented the fastest ever growing Latin genre in the United States after tejano music entered the mainstream market during its 1990s golden age.
Originally, "Billboard" based their methodology on sales surveys it sent out to record stores across the United States and by 1991 began monitoring point-of-sales compiled from Nielsen Soundscan.

Musicologist and critics have since criticized the sales data compiled from Nielsen, finding that the company only provides sales from larger music chains than from small shops that specialized in Latin music—where the majority of Latin music sales are generated.
The magazine decided to rank Latin music recordings in August 1970 under the title Hot Latin LPs, which only ranked the best-selling Latin albums in Los Angeles (Pop) and the East Coast (Salsa).
Before the chart's inception, musicians' only chart success was the Texas Latin LPs (formerly the San Antonio Latin LPs) section where regional Mexican music was more prominent.
Beginning in November 1993, "Billboard" lowered the rankings from twenty-five to fifteen positions on its Latin genre-specific charts, while the Top Latin Albums expanded to fifty titles.
From July 2001 until April 2005, the chart increased to twenty titles and then lowered back to fifteen titles.
Since 2009, the Regional Mexican Albums chart list the top twenty best-selling albums determined by sales data compiled from Neilsen Soundscan.
"Reapers" is a song by English rock band Muse.

It was released as the second promotional single from the band's seventh studio album "Drones", and was given a 7" single release, as part of Record Store Day 2016, on 16 April 2016 as the fifth and final single from Drones.

It peaked at number 75 on the French Singles Chart, 71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart, 37 on "Billboard"' s Hot Rock Songs, and became Muse's highest-charting single on "Billboard"' s Mainstream Rock Songs at number 2.
The Canadian Albums Chart is the official album sales chart in Canada.
It is compiled every Monday by U.S.-based music sales tracking company Nielsen SoundScan, and published every Tuesday by "Billboard".
The Gaon Digital Chart is a chart that ranks the best-performing singles in South Korea.
Managed by the domestic Ministry of Culture, Sports and Tourism (MCST), its data is compiled by the Korea Music Content Industry Association and published by the Gaon Music Chart.
The ranking is based collectively on each single's weekly download sales, stream count, and background music usage.
In mid-2008, the Recording Industry Association of Korea ceased publishing music sales data.
The MCST established a process to collect music sales in 2009, and began publicly publishing its data with the introduction of the Gaon Music Chart the following February.
With the creation of the Gaon Digital Chart, digital data for individual songs was provided in the country for the first time.
"Shut Me Up" is a single by Mindless Self Indulgence, released on September 12, 2006.
It is the first track on their third studio album, "You'll Rebel to Anything".
"Shut Me Up" peaked at number 7 on the "Billboard" Hot Singles Sales chart and number 1 on the Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
"Evening Wear" and "Mark David Chapman" are songs by Mindless Self Indulgence, released as a double A-side single on January 19, 2009 in the UK and January 20, 2009 in North America.
This single includes the video for "Mark David Chapman" in four different formats, directed by Mike Dahlquist, and the previously unreleased song, "Written in Cold Blood".
"Evening Wear/Mark David Chapman" peaked at number 2 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 Singles Sales chart and number 1 on the Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
The Christmas Album is the twentieth studio album by Neil Diamond and his first to feature Christmas music.
It features orchestral and choir arrangements by David Campbell.
The album reached No. 8 on the "Billboard" 200 album sales chart, No. 50 on the UK album sales chart, and No. 30 on Australian music chart.
Bardeux is a dance duo from the 1980s.
The group was formed in 1986 and consisted of Stacy "Acacia" Smith and Tairrie B.
The group's first single "Three Time Lover", went to #10 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
Tairrie B left the group to pursue a solo career and was replaced by Lisa "Jaz" Teaney.
In 1988 the group released their album "Bold as Love" and their second single "Magic Carpet Ride".
The single went to #5 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart and #81 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
The group performed the song on Club MTV.
"When We Kiss", the third single from the album, would turn out to be the group's biggest hit going all the way to #36 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The fourth single released from the album, "Bleeding Heart", was a top 40 hit on the Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
A fifth and last single release from that album, "Hold Me, Hold Me" did not chart.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What number did the song "Reapers"  by Muse reach on Switzerland's main music sales chart?
hop1_docs: Reapers (song) | "Reapers" is a song by English rock band Muse.  It was released as the second promotional single from the band's seventh studio album "Drones", and was given a 7" single release, as part of Record Store Day 2016, on 16 April 2016 as the fifth and final single from Drones.  It peaked at number 75 on the French Singles Chart, 71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart, 37 on "Billboard"' s Hot Rock Songs, and became Muse's highest-charting single on "Billboard"' s Mainstream Rock Songs at number 2.

What I Did Right | "What I Did Right" is a song recorded by American country music group Sons of the Desert.  It was released in February 2001 as the third single from the album "Change".  The song reached number 22 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart.  Lead singer Drew Womack wrote the song with Sonny LeMaire.

Oh What It Did to Me | "Oh What It Did to Me" is a song written by Jerry Crutchfield, and recorded by American country music artist Tanya Tucker.  It was released in February 1991 as the fourth single from her album "Tennessee Woman".  The song reached number 12 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart in June 1991.

Muse (Jolin Tsai album) | Muse is the twelfth studio album by Taiwanese singer Jolin Tsai ( ).  It was released on September 12, 2012, by Warner Music Taiwan.  The artistically-flavored album actually brought back all the sales that were lost in her last studio album, "Myself" (2010).  Referred to as a "pop masterpiece", the album houses many tracks that are influenced by the elements of art, poetry, and dance.  Critical and commercial response to the album was positive, it has sold more than 100,000 copies sold in Taiwan alone, and made her the best-selling female singer of the year in Taiwan.  The lead single, "The Great Artist", reached number 2 on the Hit FM Top 100 Singles of the Year.  The second track, "Dr. Jolin", reached number 10 on the chart.  The eighth track, "Wandering Poet", reached number 24 on the chart.  The album earned Tsai four Golden Melody Award nominations for Best Mandarin Album, Best Mandarin Female Singer, Best Music Video, and Song of the Year, and she finally won for Song of the Year for the lead single, "The Great Artist".  The album also earned her an MTV Europe Music Award nomination for Best Asian Act.

Hysteria (Muse song) | "Hysteria" (also known as "Hysteria (I Want It Now)" in the United States) is a song by English alternative rock band Muse and is featured on their third studio album, "Absolution".  It was also released as a single from that album on 1 December 2003 in the United Kingdom, peaking at number 17 in the UK Singles Chart (see 2003 in British music).  The song is also well known for its intricate bass line, which was voted the sixth best bass line of all time on MusicRadar.  It reached number 9 in the US on the "Billboard" Modern Rock Tracks chart.

A Song of Joy | "A Song of Joy" ("Himno de la alegría") is the title of a popular rock song by the Spanish singer and actor Miguel Ríos.  The song is set to the tune the Ninth Symphony by Ludwig van Beethoven, as arranged by Waldo de los Rios, who specialized in arranging classical music to contemporary rhythms.  The single was enormously popular in many countries in 1970 (see 1970 in music), reaching number one on music charts in Australia, Canada, Germany, Switzerland, and the Easy Listening chart in the United States.  On the U.S. pop chart, the song peaked at number 14 and was the only Top 40 hit for Ríos.  In the United Kingdom, the song reached number 16 on the British pop chart.  In Germany, the song is the most successful pop hymn ever.

Too Much (Elvis Presley song) | "Too Much" is a #1 song recorded in a hit version by Elvis Presley and published by Elvis Presley Music in 1956.  It was written by Bernard Weinman and Lee Rosenberg.  It was first recorded in 1954 by Bernard Hardison on Republic Records.  Elvis Presley recorded the song in September 1956 and first performed it on January 6, 1957 on CBS-TV's "The Ed Sullivan Show."  Released as a single, Presley's "Too Much" reached number one on both the Cashbox and Billboard sales charts and went to number three on the R&B chart.  The single peaked at number two on the then-named Top 100 chart, the main Billboard pop chart.
hop1_titles: Oh What It Did to Me
response.summary_1: Passage [1] reports that "Reapers" "peaked at ... 71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart." To answer the question, confirm the bridge identity: verify that the "Swiss Hitparade singles chart" (aka Swiss Singles Chart) is "Switzerland's main music sales chart" — retrieve an authoritative source stating that equivalence.
response.hop2_query: Swiss Hitparade Swiss Singles Chart main music sales chart official
hop2_docs: Swiss Hitparade | The Swiss Hitparade (German: Schweizer Hitparade ) are Switzerland's main music sales charts.  The charts are a record of the highest-selling singles and albums in various genres in Switzerland.

Tony T. | Neal Antone Dyer better known by the stage name Tony T. is a British singer, rapper and DJ of Jamaican origin, best known as the frontman of German dance music projects Beat System and R.I.O..  His best known hit with R.I.O. was "Turn This Club Around" that reached number 3 in German Singles Chart and topped the Swiss Schweizer Hitparade chart, and was certified gold in both charts.

Reapers (song) | "Reapers" is a song by English rock band Muse.  It was released as the second promotional single from the band's seventh studio album "Drones", and was given a 7" single release, as part of Record Store Day 2016, on 16 April 2016 as the fifth and final single from Drones.  It peaked at number 75 on the French Singles Chart, 71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart, 37 on "Billboard"' s Hot Rock Songs, and became Muse's highest-charting single on "Billboard"' s Mainstream Rock Songs at number 2.

List of number-one singles (Finland) | In this page are contained all the lists of number-one singles from the Official Finnish Charts, the official Finnish music charts that began in 1989.  Musiikkituottajat – IFPI Finland, the association of Finnish music industry, publishes currently three separate singles charts: the Official Finnish Singles Chart ("Suomen virallinen singlelista") which takes into account both physical singles and digital tracks (singles, promotional singles and non-singles), the Official Finnish Download Chart ("Suomen virallinen latauslista"), which since February 2007 has listed tracks accordingly to their digital sales, and the Official Finnish Airplay Chart, which since November 24, 2013, has listed tracks accordingly to their airplay.  On April 5, 2012, the Singles Chart began to include music streaming in addition to digital and physical sales.

Swiss Lady | "Swiss Lady" was the Swiss entry in the Eurovision Song Contest 1977, performed in German by Pepe Lienhard Band.  It remains the only Swiss entry to have reached no. 1 on the official Swiss Single Chart.

ARIA Charts | The ARIA Charts are the main Australian music sales charts, issued weekly by the Australian Recording Industry Association.  The charts are a record of the highest selling singles and albums in various genres in Australia.  ARIA became the official Australian music chart in June 1988, succeeding the Kent Music Report which had been Australia's national charts since 1974.

2005 in Swiss music | 2005 was a big year for Swiss music, with the charts becoming steadier yet less predictable than they had been in previous years.  The year saw many chart debuts from both Swiss and international acts, and saw two novelty songs share a combined total of over ten weeks at the singles chart number one spot.  Internationally, the Swiss also saw Vanilla Ninja take the country to their best Eurovision Song Contest position in twelve years.
response.summary_2: "Reapers" reached number 71 on Switzerland's main music sales chart. The Swiss Hitparade is Switzerland's main music sales chart (passage [1]); Reapers: peaked at #71 on the Swiss Hitparade singles chart (passage [3]).
